title: AP U.S. History DBQ Practice Exam 08

questions.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which public health and consumer-protection concerns changed the role of government in American society from 1890 to 1920.
Document 1
Author/Origin: Original simulated report by a municipal health inspector, New York City, 1894; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Government inspection report excerpt.
“Several tenement blocks contain rear buildings with little light or air. Privies overflow after rain, and milk is stored in rooms where sick children sleep. Landlords state that rents cannot support improvements, yet disease does not remain within one hallway. The city must treat housing as a public health matter.”
Sourcing Cues: The report reflects the perspective of a local official arguing for expanded municipal responsibility over housing and sanitation.
Document 2
Author/Origin: Described neighborhood health map created for this practice exam, Chicago, circa 1902.
Source Type: Described map.
The map shows several crowded immigrant and working-class neighborhoods near stockyards, rail lines, and factories. Shaded areas mark high rates of tuberculosis and infant mortality. Dots identify public baths, settlement houses, milk stations, and clinics. Wealthier residential districts on the edge of the map show fewer shaded areas and more open space.
Sourcing Cues: The map connects disease patterns to industrial urban geography, class inequality, and reform institutions.
Document 3
Author/Origin: Original simulated statement from a meatpacking worker to a state labor investigator, Illinois, 1905; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Testimony-style excerpt.
“The floor is slick, the knives move fast, and spoiled pieces are trimmed until they look fit enough to sell. A man who complains is told there are others waiting outside. The public sees a wrapped package, not the blood, speed, and fear that put it there.”
Sourcing Cues: The testimony reflects the perspective of an industrial worker linking workplace conditions to consumer safety.
Document 4
Author/Origin: Original simulated editorial from a national women’s club journal, 1906; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Reform editorial excerpt.
“Mothers are expected to guard the health of the household, but no mother can inspect a cannery, test every bottle of medicine, or trace every cup of milk to its source. Honest labels and public inspection are not attacks on business; they are protections for families.”
Sourcing Cues: The editorial reflects middle-class women reformers’ use of maternal and household authority to support regulation.
Document 5
Author/Origin: Summary of federal food and drug legislation, Washington, D.C., 1906.
Source Type: Government policy summary.
The law prohibited the interstate sale of foods and drugs that were adulterated or falsely labeled. Supporters argued that national markets required national standards. Critics warned that federal inspection could burden producers and expand government authority over private business.
Sourcing Cues: The summary reflects federal responses to consumer-protection concerns in a national industrial economy.
Document 6
Author/Origin: Infant health data table created for this practice exam, selected U.S. cities, 1900–1918.
Source Type: Data table.
The table is titled “Selected Public Health Reforms and Infant Mortality.”
City | Common reforms adopted by 1918 | Reported trend
New York | milk stations, visiting nurses, tenement regulation | infant mortality declined
Chicago | sanitation campaigns, settlement clinics, food inspection | infant mortality declined
Philadelphia | clean milk campaigns, public health education | infant mortality declined
Smaller industrial cities | reforms uneven or delayed | mixed results
A note beneath the table states: “Public health improvements varied by city, funding, enforcement, and neighborhood access.”
Sourcing Cues: The table emphasizes both reform gains and uneven implementation.
Document 7
Author/Origin: Original simulated statement from a small food manufacturer to a business association, Ohio, 1912; based on historical context, not an actual quotation.
Source Type: Business association statement.
“We do not defend fraud. But each new inspection form, label rule, and factory standard costs money that large corporations can absorb more easily than small firms. Reformers say regulation protects the public, but poorly designed regulation may also drive independent producers out of business.”
Sourcing Cues: The statement reflects business concerns about costs, competition, and the uneven effects of regulation.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 7
Thesis / Claim — 1 point: Earns the point for a historically defensible claim evaluating the extent to which public health and consumer-protection concerns changed the role of government in American society from 1890 to 1920. The thesis must make an argument, such as that these concerns significantly expanded municipal, state, and federal regulation of housing, food, medicine, sanitation, and industry, though enforcement remained uneven and contested by business interests.
Contextualization — 1 point: Earns the point by describing broader relevant context, such as urbanization, industrialization, tenement growth, national markets, Progressive reform, muckraking journalism, women’s reform activism, immigration, scientific management, or the expansion of administrative government.
Evidence from the Documents — 2 points: Earns 1 point for accurately using content from at least three documents to address the prompt. Earns 2 points for using content from at least six documents to support a coherent argument about housing regulation, urban disease, industrial food production, women’s reform activism, federal consumer legislation, public health campaigns, or business opposition.
Evidence Beyond the Documents — 1 point: Earns the point for using at least one specific, relevant piece of outside evidence not found in the documents, such as The Jungle, Pure Food and Drug Act, Meat Inspection Act, settlement houses, Jane Addams, tenement reform, muckrakers, Florence Kelley, public health departments, child labor reform, or state factory inspection laws.
Sourcing / Analysis of Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by explaining how or why the point of view, purpose, audience, or historical situation of at least three documents is relevant to the argument. For example, a student might analyze the health inspector’s municipal reform purpose, the map’s use of data to justify intervention, the worker’s view of unsafe production, the women’s club journal’s maternalist audience, the federal law summary’s national-market context, or the manufacturer’s concern about regulatory costs.
Complexity — 1 point: Earns the point by demonstrating a nuanced argument, such as explaining that reform expanded government authority while relying on local enforcement; showing that consumer protection connected private households to public regulation; distinguishing public health, labor safety, and business regulation; or recognizing that reforms could protect consumers while burdening small producers or leaving poor communities underserved.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong DBQ would likely argue that public health and consumer-protection concerns significantly expanded the role of government in American society from 1890 to 1920. Students should connect urban disease, industrial food production, national markets, women’s reform activism, and Progressive Era regulation. Effective document groupings include urban housing and disease, industrial production and consumer safety, women reformers and household protection, federal regulation, public health data, and business resistance. Relevant outside evidence could include The Jungle, Pure Food and Drug Act, Meat Inspection Act, muckrakers, settlement houses, Jane Addams, Florence Kelley, tenement reform, or state factory inspection laws.
